elements.sent-2411-018: To overcome the limitations of existing methods, this paper introduces, to the best of our knowledge, the first single-model attribution technique in an open-world setting.
elements.sent-2411-019: Here, single-model attribution determines whether a sample was generated by a specific vocoder of interest, and an open-world setting corresponds to the system's ability to distinguish between speech signals generated by a known vocoder and inputs from any previously unseen model.

elements.sent-2411-023: In this section, we review previous research on deepfake detection and revisit the concept of generative model fingerprints as they have been proposed in the computer vision domain for generative adversarial networks (GANs).
elements.sent-2411-024: The study of deepfake detection has two main fields of research: spoofing detection systems and vocoder attribution methods.
elements.sent-2411-025: Spoofing detection systems generally consist of two components: an acoustic feature extractor and a classifier.
elements.sent-2411-026: The acoustic feature extractor is designed to extract relevant characteristics from the raw audio input into a form that a classifier can effectively analyze.

elements.sent-2411-053: In this section, building upon the fingerprinting methodology for GAN-generated images, we present an approach for extracting fingerprints of generative models for audio and employing them for model attribution.
elements.sent-2411-054: While applying the methodology may sound straightforward at first sight, we are confronted with two major challenges.
elements.sent-2411-055: First, note that the length of audio waveforms is typically not unified.
elements.sent-2411-056: Since the fingerprint however is based on averaging filter-based residuals, it is necessary to find a representation of audio waveforms that is independent of the audio length.
elements.sent-2411-057: To remedy this, we choose the average energy (in dB) per frequency bin $E(X)$ of the audio $X$, whose size is given by the number of frequency bins of the spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-058: The second challenge is to find a suitable filter capable of removing noise unrelated to the content of the audio.
elements.sent-2411-059: We found two filters to work particularly well: 1) EnCodec~\cite{defossez_2023_TMLR , which corresponds to a deep autoencoder developed for compressing audio, and 2) a low-pass filter that attenuates signal components above a specified cutoff frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Specifically, we propose to estimate a fingerprint based on samples $X_1, \dots, X_N$ by \begin{equation}\hat{F} := \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N R_i \enspace, \text{where } R_i:= E(X_i) - f(E(X_i)) \enspace ,\end{equation} where $f$ either corresponds to the low-pass filter or the concatenation of the EnCodec encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-061: Figure~\ref{fig:fingerprints shows the fingerprints extracted for some vocoders.}
elements.sent-2411-062: As an alternative to the correlation-based scoring we introduce scoring based on the Mahalanobis distance to the fingerprint, that is defined by \begin{equation}s_{\operatorname{md}}(X_{\operatorname{test}}, \hat{F}) := - \sqrt{ (R_{\operatorname{test}} - \hat{F} )^\top S^{-1} (R_{\operatorname{test}} - \hat{F} ) } \enspace ,\end{equation} where $S^{-1}$ denotes the inverse of the empirical covariance matrix of the training residuals.
elements.sent-2411-063: In contrast to $s_{\operatorname{cor }$, $s_{\operatorname{md }$ incorporates the variance and covariance between different energy bins of the residuals and corrects the score accordingly.}}

elements.sent-2411-186: With speech generation models achieving remarkable realism and ease of use---or misuse---the need for spoofing detection and vocoder attribution in an open- or closed-world setting is growing quickly.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our approach, using either EnCodec or a low-pass filter for fingerprint extraction, shows excellent performance across all three tasks.
elements.sent-2411-188: For single-model attribution in an open-world setting, it accurately distinguishes samples of a target vocoder from those generated by unknown vocoders or drawn from real audio files, achieving near-perfect AUROC scores.
elements.sent-2411-189: In the closed-world setting, it reliably identifies the vocoder that generated a given sample, outperforming baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-190: Finally, for fake vs.~real detection, it effectively differentiates synthetic speech from real audio, even without dataset augmentation, demonstrating robustness and potential for further improvement.
elements.sent-2411-191: Additionally, our single-model attribution framework proves effective in noisy conditions, maintaining good performance even with an SNR above \SI{15 {\decibel .}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Thus, our fingerprint approach adds a simple but highly effective technique to the model attribution and deepfake detection toolbox.
elements.sent-2411-193: Future research can explore how different vocoders produce distinct audio fingerprints and assessing the robustness of our attribution technique against further types of variations, including compression artifacts, different bandwidths, or bit rates.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-194: As speech generation technologies continue to advance in quality and accessibility, the risk of malicious use cases, including impersonation, misinformation, and spoofing, increases rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-195: This work addresses this threat by introducing a simple, training-free, yet effective approach for detecting AI-generated speech and attributing it to its source model.
elements.sent-2411-196: Specifically, we tackle three key tasks: (1) single-model attribution in an open-world setting, where the goal is to determine whether a given audio sample was generated by a specific target neural speech synthesis system (with access only to data from that system); (2) multi-model attribution in a closed-world setting, where the objective is to identify the generating system from a known pool of candidates; and last but not least (3) detection of synthetic versus real speech.
elements.sent-2411-197: Our approach leverages standardized average residuals—the difference between an input audio signal and its filtered version using either a low-pass filter or the EnCodec audio autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-198: We demonstrate that these residuals consistently capture artifacts introduced by diverse speech synthesis systems, serving as distinctive, model-agnostic fingerprints for attribution.
elements.sent-2411-199: Across extensive experiments, our approach achieves AUROC scores exceeding 99\% in most scenarios, evaluated on augmented benchmark datasets that pair real speech with synthetic audio generated by multiple synthesis systems.
elements.sent-2411-200: In addition, our robustness analysis underscores the method's ability to maintain high performance even in the presence of moderate additive noise.
elements.sent-2411-201: Due to its simplicity, efficiency, and strong generalization across speech synthesis systems and languages, this technique offers a practical tool for digital forensics and security applications.
elements.sent-2411-202: This issue is particularly relevant today because public and open-source tools enable even unskilled attackers to synthesize highly realistic voice samples that closely resemble human speech~\cite{yan-etal-2023-espnet, ravanelli_2024_speechbrain .}
elements.sent-2411-203: Although these technologies offer numerous advantages, such as improving accessibility for speech-impaired individuals~\cite{Metzger_2023_NatureJournal and supporting multilingual communication~\cite{badlani2023_interspeech , they also pose several security risks.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Most pressingly, synthetic speech poses a growing threat to security by enabling spoofing attacks on Automatic Speaker Verification (ASV) systems, as well as impersonation and circumvention of other voice-controlled systems.
elements.sent-2411-205: These threats can be exploited in various harmful ways, such as disinformation campaigns~\cite{Bontridder_Poullet_2021_article , biometric spoofing attacks~\cite{Gupta_2024_EURASIP , misleading juries in criminal trials through forged audio evidence~\cite{Delfino_2023_LawJournal , or cloning voices to commit identity fraud~\cite{Dongyang_2022_ICASSP, klapsas_2022_interspeech .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: The emergence of synthetic audio as a vector for impersonation and manipulation presents a direct challenge to the security and trustworthiness of modern communication systems.
elements.sent-2411-207: While deepfake detection models offer a line of defense, many require costly supervised training and large labeled datasets—limiting their scalability.
elements.sent-2411-208: This highlights the need for more lightweight or training-free attribution methods.
elements.sent-2411-209: This objective is reflected in several well-established benchmark challenges such as Automatic Speaker Verification Spoofing (ASVspoof)~\cite{wang_2024_asvspoof5 and Audio Deepfake Detection (ADD)~\cite{Yi_2023_IJCAI .}}
elements.sent-2411-210: However, an essential gap remains: model attribution, also known as algorithm recognition, that is, the identification of the specific data-generating model behind a synthetic speech signal, is still underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-211: This attribution plays a vital role in real-world forensics and security analysis, where linking synthetic speech to its source model can aid in identifying malicious actors, tracing the origin of disinformation campaigns, enforcing intellectual property rights, and strengthening cybersecurity threat analysis.
elements.sent-2411-212: Recent studies have explored multiclass classification approaches to attribute speech samples to specific synthesis systems.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, these models operate in a closed-world setting~\cite{Xinrui_2022_DeepfakeWorkshop, Junlong_2024_ICASSP , which means that they can only recognize systems seen during training.}
elements.sent-2411-214: As a result, they do not generalize to new models without costly retraining, which presents a major limitation given the rapid evolution of synthetic speech technologies.
elements.sent-2411-215: This paper presents a novel, efficient, and training-free approach to speech synthesis system attribution as well as synthesized speech detection that can be directly applied to cybersecurity and forensic workflows.
elements.sent-2411-216: To the best of our knowledge, we introduce the first single-model attribution technique in an open-world setting, i.e., a technique allowing to identify whether a speech sample was generated by a specific model while distinguishing it from any unseen speech synthesis systems or even from real speech.
elements.sent-2411-217: Notably, our method only requires samples from one target model and does not require training a costly machine learning classifier, making it highly adaptable to newly evolving synthetic speech technologies.
elements.sent-2411-218: In addition to open-world attribution, our method represents an easy and cheap approach to closed-world model classification and synthetic speech detection.
elements.sent-2411-219: Therefore, our method provides a unified solution to multiple threat scenarios, and notably, it outperforms state-of-the-art strategies in all these settings.
elements.sent-2411-220: Precisely, our key contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-221: Discovery of model-specific speech fingerprints: We demonstrate that neural speech synthesis systems leave behind consistent, model-specific artifacts in generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-222: These subtle residual patterns act as reliable fingerprints and persist across diverse samples.
elements.sent-2411-223: Proposal of a unified attribution and detection framework: We propose a lightweight, training-free method that exploits these fingerprints to perform both model attribution (in single- and multi-model settings) and synthetic speech detection.
elements.sent-2411-224: Extensive empirical evaluation: We evaluate our technique on a broad variety of datasets and speech generation systems and demonstrate its robustness to real-world noise.
elements.sent-2411-225: Our approach remains effective under moderate background noise, highlighting its suitability for forensic and security applications in realistic, uncontrolled environments.
elements.sent-2411-226: By addressing the attribution and detection of synthetic speech as a response to malicious misuse of generative models, this work advances the development of lightweight, deployable defenses against speech synthesis attacks, contributing directly to applied cybersecurity.
elements.sent-2411-227: The rise of advanced speech synthesis models—both text-to-speech (TTS)~\cite{wang_2017_interspeech and voice conversion (VC)~\cite{kaneko_2017_interspeech —poses a significant threat to the integrity and trustworthiness of modern communication systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: These models can be used to impersonate individuals, generate persuasive misinformation, and compromise authentication systems.
elements.sent-2411-229: TTS systems generate speech from arbitrary text inputs using a target speaker’s voice, while VC systems modify a source speaker’s voice to resemble a target speaker’s voice, preserving the linguistic content.
elements.sent-2411-230: From a security and forensic perspective, it is critical to develop techniques that can reliably detect AI-generated audio.
elements.sent-2411-231: But detection alone is not sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-232: It is also of great relevance to be able to attribute synthetic audio to its generative source.
elements.sent-2411-233: This enables defenders to (1) trace the origin of misinformation or impersonation attacks, (2) hold the creators of malicious content accountable, and (3) design targeted countermeasures against specific synthesis models—for example, in high-security environments (like banking voice authentication or governmental communications), systems might deploy lightweight model-specific classifiers to flag suspicious audio in real-time, apply adaptive thresholds based on the known behavior of the synthesis model, or use the model’s outputs to adversarially train more robust detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-234: It is also of importance from the perspective of the model owner, since it can be employed to detect model theft or the illegal usage of a model.
elements.sent-2411-235: Attacker model: We assume a non-adaptive adversary with access to one or more generative speech models.
elements.sent-2411-236: These may be public (e.g., open-source) or proprietary systems, and the attacker can generate synthetic audio, which is transmitted losslessly to the target system.
elements.sent-2411-237: The adversary does not know which detection or attribution mechanism will be used by the defender.
elements.sent-2411-238: Defender model: The defender aims to detect or attribute potentially fake audio samples.
elements.sent-2411-239: Their capabilities vary across scenarios—ranging from having access to generated data from only one synthesis model to having data from multiple generators.
elements.sent-2411-240: Attribution relies on the audio samples alone, without knowledge of the internal workings of the generative models.
elements.sent-2411-241: We define the attribution problem along two dimensions:.
elements.sent-2411-242: Single-model vs.~multi-model: Data of how many synthesis models is available to the defender for constructing the attribution technique?.
elements.sent-2411-243: Open-world vs.~closed-world: Is the synthesis model used by the attacker assumed to be included in the construction phase of the attribution method, or can it be a previously unseen (unknown) model?.
elements.sent-2411-244: These axes define three core scenarios illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:workflow_model_attribution and detailed below.}

elements.sent-2411-256: This section reviews existing research on the detection and attribution of synthetic audio—commonly known as audio deepfake detection—focusing on approaches suitable for practical, automated identification of generative model outputs.
elements.sent-2411-257: It also revisits the concept of generative model fingerprints, originally proposed in the computer vision domain for generative adversarial networks (GANs).
elements.sent-2411-258: Research in deepfake detection spans anti-spoofing techniques and synthesis system attribution methods.
elements.sent-2411-259: These systems typically consist of an acoustic feature extractor and a classifier.
elements.sent-2411-260: The acoustic feature extractor is designed to extract relevant characteristics from the raw audio input, which are then forwarded to the classifier.
elements.sent-2411-261: Examples of spectral features include Linear-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (LFCC)~\cite{Xinhui_2011_ASRU , Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC)~\cite{Xinhui_2011_ASRU , Constant Q~Cepstral Coefficients~\cite{Todisco_2017_JournalCSL , and long-term variable Q~transform~\cite{Li_2022_JournalDSP .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The classifier is then trained on these acoustic features to distinguish real from synthetic audio samples.
elements.sent-2411-263: Classifier models that have demonstrated effectiveness for this task are based on Gaussian mixture models~\cite{ji_2017_interspeech or various types of neural networks like X-vector~\cite{Snyder_2018_SpokenLR , Light Convolutional Neural Networks (LCNNs)~\cite{wu_2020_interspeech, Akash_2020_IEEEJournal , Residual Networks (ResNets)~\cite{alzantot_2019_interspeech, yang_2019_interspeech, Juntae_2023_ICASSP , Squeeze-Excitation and Residual Networks (SE-ResNets)~\cite{lai_2019_interspeech, zhang_2021_interspeech, Hemlata_2021_ICASSP , Graph Attention Networks~\cite{tak_2021_interspeech, Jee_2022_ICASSP , Differentiable Architecture Search ~\cite{ge_2021_interspeech , and Transformers~\cite{Xiaohui_2023_ICASSP .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-264: In contrast, model attribution aims to identify the specific generative synthesis system responsible for producing the synthetic audio samples.
elements.sent-2411-265: Deep learning-based synthesis systems outperform traditional parametric and concatenative methods in both speech quality and flexibility, leveraging neural networks to generate high-fidelity speech with improved prosody and speaker similarity.
elements.sent-2411-266: Among the most successful synthesis system approaches are GAN-based models~\cite{frank_2021_NeurIPS , which leverage adversarial training to generate high-quality, low-latency speech; Flow-based models~\cite{Prenger_2019_ICASSP , which utilize invertible transformations for efficient waveform synthesis; Diffusion-based models~\cite{huang_2022_IJCAI , which refine noise into speech through iterative processes, achieving state-of-the-art quality at the cost of higher computational requirements; and Hybrid signal processing-deep learning models~\cite{wang_19_SSW , which combine traditional signal processing techniques with deep learning to improve interpretability and control in speech synthesis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Frank and Schönherr~\cite{frank_2021_NeurIPS observed that speech generated by GAN-based synthesis systems exhibits artifacts, particularly in the higher frequency ranges, that are characteristic of the underlying generation method.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Yan et al.\cite{Xinrui_2022_DeepfakeWorkshop utilized LFCC features and a ResNet classifier to detect audio produced by eight different synthetic speech models, while Li et al.\cite{Fan_2024_ACM applied MFCCs and a lightweight CNN to classify samples generated by four such systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-269: Similarly, Deng et al.~\cite{Junlong_2024_ICASSP employed Mel spectrograms and trained a neural network using contrastive learning to identify synthetic speech generated by six distinct models.}
elements.sent-2411-270: Prior efforts, while effective in closed-world settings, struggle with generalization to unknown generative models, often due to overfitting or limited training diversity.
elements.sent-2411-271: An example of recent work addressing this limitation is the third track of the ADD 2023 challenge~\cite{Yi_2023_IJCAI , which includes an open-world model attribution task.}
elements.sent-2411-272: However, it differs from our approach in three key ways.
elements.sent-2411-273: In contrast, this paper emphasizes the open-world attribution task while also considering the closed-world setting, supporting practical use in cybersecurity operations such as fake audio detection and attribution of generative model sources.
elements.sent-2411-274: In parallel, fake speech prevention has emerged as a proactive line of defense, aiming to stop unauthorized synthesis before it occurs.
elements.sent-2411-275: For instance, this can be achieved by introducing subtle perturbations to a target speaker’s audio before it is accessed by an attacker~\cite{Huang_2021_SLT , or by altering the audio in the frequency domain prior to sharing or distribution~\cite{Zihao_2023_ACSAC .}}
elements.sent-2411-276: While such approaches are effective in protecting user privacy and mitigating synthesis attacks, they differ fundamentally from model attribution.
elements.sent-2411-277: Prevention techniques aim to block the synthesis process, whereas model attribution seeks to identify the specific system responsible for already generated synthetic audio.
elements.sent-2411-278: As such, attribution complements both prevention and detection strategies by enabling post-hoc forensic and investigative capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-279: The concept of model-specific fingerprints was initially explored by Marra et al.~\cite{marra2019gans in the context of image generation using GANs.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Their idea is based on the assumption that any artificially generated image $x$ decomposes into its content $I(x)$ and a fingerprint $F$ that is unrelated to the image semantics but specific to the model, that is, $x = I(x) + F$ for every $x$ generated from the model.
elements.sent-2411-281: To extract the fingerprint, they assumed that a suitable image filter $f$ is capable of removing the fingerprint, such that $f(x) \approx I(x)$, and therefore $R := x - f(x) \approx F$.
elements.sent-2411-282: Given a set of generated samples $x_1, \dots, x_N$, the sample-wise residuals are defined by $R_i:= x_i - f(x_i)$ and $F$ is estimated by $\hat{F := \frac{1 {N}\sum_{i=1 ^N R_i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-283: For inference, i.e., for checking whether a test sample $x_{\operatorname{test }$ contains a fingerprint similar to $\hat{F}$, they first compute its residual $R_{\operatorname{test }$ as above and then assign the correlation score $s_{\operatorname{cor (x_{\operatorname{test ; \, \hat{F ) := \langle \tilde{R _{\operatorname{test \, , \, \tilde{F \rangle \in [-1, 1]$, where $\tilde{R _{\operatorname{test }$ and $\tilde{F}$ denote the zero-mean and unit-norm versions of $R_{\operatorname{test }$ and $\hat{F}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Having a set of $m$ different generative models, and therefore, a set of corresponding fingerprints $\hat{F _1, \dots, \hat{F _m$, one attributes a sample to the $j$th model, where $j=\argmax_{i\in [m] s_{\operatorname{cor (x_{\operatorname{test ; \, \hat{F _i)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: We propose an audio fingerprinting methodology for model attribution, inspired by approaches developed for GAN-generated images, which is largely unexplored in the audio domain.
elements.sent-2411-286: Unlike images, audio signals pose unique challenges for fingerprint extraction.
elements.sent-2411-287: This task presents two main challenges that distinguish audio fingerprinting from its image counterpart:.
elements.sent-2411-288: Variable-length: Unlike fixed-size images, audio signals vary in duration, but fingerprint extraction requires a fixed-size representation for consistent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-289: Content-preserving filtering: While image fingerprints rely on spatial filtering, audio requires filters that suppress content-related features such as speech semantics and phonetics, while preserving subtle generative model-specific artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-290: Our pipeline addresses these challenges by first converting each audio sample to an average spectral energy representation and then applying suitable filters to extract residuals, which are averaged to form a fingerprint as detailed in the following.
elements.sent-2411-291: To overcome the length variability of the audio signals, we transform each signal into a fixed-size representation that summarizes its spectral content.
elements.sent-2411-292: We apply the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), which converts a time-domain signal into a time-frequency representation known as a spectrogram.
elements.sent-2411-293: First, let \( x^{(i) \) be a discrete-time audio signal of arbitrary length, representing the \( i \)-th sample.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Since the frequency content of audio signals typically changes over time, applying a single global Fourier Transform would only provide an average frequency representation over the entire signal, thereby losing important temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-295: To capture how the frequency components evolve, we divide the signal into overlapping short-time frames \( x^{(i) (k, t) \), where:.}
elements.sent-2411-296: Frames are often overlapped (e.g., 25\% overlap) to improve temporal continuity and reduce artifacts from abrupt frame boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-297: Therefore, the total number of frames \( T^{(i) \) depends on the signal length, the sampling rate, the frame length \( L \), and the hop size (i.e., the number of samples between consecutive frames).}
elements.sent-2411-298: Second, to mitigate spectral leakage at frame boundaries, each frame is multiplied by a smooth window function \( \omega(k) \), such as a Hann or Hamming window: \begin{equation}x^{(i)}_\omega(k, t) = x^{(i)}(k, t) \cdot \omega(k) \enspace .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-299: This smooths the signal at the boundaries of each frame before applying the Fourier Transform.
elements.sent-2411-300: We then apply the Discrete Fourier Transform to each windowed frame: \begin{equation}X^{(i)}_{\mathrm{DFT}}(f, t) = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x^{(i)}_\omega(k, t) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k f / L} \enspace ,\end{equation} where \( f \in \{0, \dots, L-1\} \) is the frequency bin index, and \( X^{(i) _{\mathrm{DFT (f, t) \in \mathbb{C \) is the complex frequency spectrum of frame \( t \).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}X^{(i)}_{DFT}(f, t) = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x^{(i)}_\omega(k, t) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k f / L} \enspace ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-301: To obtain the spectrogram, we take the magnitude of the complex spectrum: \begin{equation}{S}^{(i)}_X(f, t) = \left| X^{(i)}_{\mathrm{DFT}}(f, t) \right| \enspace .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-302: The resulting spectrogram \( {S ^{(i) _X \in \mathbb{R ^{F \times T^{(i) \) represents the signal's energy distribution over time and frequency, where \( F \) is the number of unique frequency bins.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-303: We apply a logarithmic transformation to map power values to decibels: \begin{equation}\mathcal{S}^{(i)}_X(f, t) = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( {S}^{(i)}_X(f, t) \right) \enspace .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-304: To obtain a fixed-size representation independent of audio length, we compute the average energy per frequency bin: \begin{equation}E_{x^{(i)}}[f] := \frac{1}{T^{(i)}} \sum_{t=1}^{T^{(i)}} \mathcal{S}^{(i)}_X(f, t) \enspace ,\end{equation} yielding a vector \( E_{x^{(i) \in \mathbb{R ^{F \) that summarizes the spectral energy distribution across time.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-305: This time-frequency representation forms the basis for our subsequent fingerprint estimation method that requires a fixed-size input.
elements.figure-2411-013: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/fig_paper_fingerprints.pdf}} \caption{ Extracted fingerprints \( \hat{\mathcal{F}} \) from four speech synthesis systems, computed as the average residuals (Equation~\ref{residuals}) between the average spectral energy \( E_{x^{(i)}} \) of generated audio samples and their EnCodec-filtered counterparts \( E_{f(x^{(i)})} \), highlighting model-specific spectral characteristics. } \label{fig:fingerprints}
elements.sent-2411-306: To isolate generative artifacts, we apply content-preserving filters to the audio signal before computing the residuals of our spectral energy representation.
elements.sent-2411-307: We investigate two types of filtering approaches: (1) neural compression using Encodec, and (2) spectral filtering, including low-pass, high-pass, band-stop, and band-pass filters.
elements.sent-2411-308: EnCodec is a neural audio compression model based on a quantized latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-309: We use a pre-trained causal model operating at 24 kHz on monophonic audio, trained on a variety of audio data.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/facebookresearch/encodec .. It consists of:.}}
elements.sent-2411-310: An encoder that transforms the input waveform into a latent vector \( \mathbf{w \),.}
elements.sent-2411-311: A quantizer that maps \( \mathbf{w \) to a discretized version \( \mathbf{w _q \),.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: A decoder that reconstructs the waveform from \( \mathbf{w _q \).}
elements.sent-2411-313: This architecture preserves the overall content while discarding fine-grained details, making it suitable for suppressing speech synthesis-specific artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-314: As an alternative to neural compression, we apply finite impulse response (FIR) filtering to manipulate the spectral characteristics of the audio signal in a controlled and interpretable manner.
elements.sent-2411-315: By selectively preserving or attenuating different frequency bands—such as low, high, or mid frequencies—we aim to suppress content-related information while retaining generative artifacts introduced by the synthesis model.
elements.sent-2411-316: An FIR filter operates by convolving the input signal with a set of learned or designed filter coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-317: Given a discrete-time input signal \( x^{(i) = \{x^{(i) _1, x^{(i) _2, \dots, x^{(i) _s\} \), the filtered output \( y^{(i) = \{y^{(i) _1, y^{(i) _2, \dots, y^{(i) _s\} \) is obtained via convolution: \begin{equation}y^{(i)}_n = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} h_k \cdot x^{(i)}_{n-k}, \quad \text{for } n = 1, \dots, s \enspace ,\end{equation} where \( h_k \) are the filter coefficients, and \( K \) is the filter order, i.e., the number of coefficients in the FIR filter.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: This corresponds to a standard 1D convolution operation with one input and one output channel.
elements.sent-2411-320: By choosing appropriate coefficients, we can implement low-pass, high-pass, band-stop, or band-pass behavior depending on which frequency bands we aim to isolate or suppress.
elements.sent-2411-321: This enables a flexible residual extraction strategy that highlights synthesis-specific artifacts while reducing the influence of linguistic content and speaker identity.
elements.sent-2411-322: Given a set of \( N \) audio signals \( \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1 ^N \), we estimate the generative model’s fingerprint \( \hat{\mathcal{F \) by averaging the residuals between the original and filtered average energy representations: \begin{equation}\hat{\mathcal{F}} := \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \mathcal{R}^{(i)} \enspace , \quad \text{where } \mathcal{R}^{(i)} := E_{x^{(i)}} - E_{f(x^{(i)})} \enspace . \label{residuals}\end{equation} Here, \( f(\cdot) \) denotes the chosen filter (e.g., EnCodec or spectral filtering), and \( \mathcal{R ^{(i) \in \mathbb{R ^F \) is the residual vector for signal \( x^{(i) \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-323: Figure~\ref{fig:fingerprints exemplarily illustrates the EnCodec-based fingerprints for four different speech synthesis systems.}
elements.sent-2411-324: Each fingerprint represents the standardized average residual energy distribution across frequencies, computed from the residual spectrograms.
elements.sent-2411-325: As seen in the figure, the fingerprints exhibit distinct patterns, highlighting that different synthesis systems leave characteristic traces in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-326: These differences form the basis for discriminating among models in attribution tasks.
elements.sent-2411-327: As an alternative to the correlation-based scoring method \( s_{\operatorname{cor \) defined in Section~\ref{subsec:related_work_fingerprint , we propose a scoring method based on the Mahalanobis distance, which accounts for correlations between features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-328: Given a test sample \( x_{\mathrm{test \), we compare its residual vector \( \mathcal{R _{\mathrm{test \) to the fingerprint \( \hat{\mathcal{F \) via: \begin{equation}s_{\operatorname{md}}(\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{test}}, \hat{\mathcal{F}}) := \sqrt{ (\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{test}} - \hat{\mathcal{F}} )^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{test}} - \hat{\mathcal{F}} ) } \enspace ,\end{equation} where \( \Sigma^{-1 \) is the inverse covariance matrix of the training residuals.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-329: This distance accounts for feature variance and correlation, enabling more robust attribution.
elements.sent-2411-330: It has proven effective in tasks like out-of-domain detection~\cite{Podolskiy_2021_AAAI, Behrendt_2024_journal .}
elements.sent-2411-331: While the correlation score measures the alignment between a test residual and a fingerprint—where higher values indicate stronger attribution—the Mahalanobis distance is defined such that lower distances imply higher similarity.
elements.sent-2411-332: Thus, for attribution, we can either maximize \( s_{\operatorname{cor \) to assign \( x_{\mathrm{test \) to the closest fingerprint in terms of Pearson correlation, or minimize \(s_{\operatorname{md }\) to assign \( x_{\mathrm{test \) to the closest fingerprint in terms of Mahalanobis distance.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-333: All experiments are conducted using publicly available speech synthesis systems and benchmark baselines.
elements.sent-2411-334: Each experiment is repeated five times to ensure statistical reliability, and we report the average results across runs.
elements.sent-2411-335: In all cases, the test sets consist of unseen real and synthetic samples to reliably assess generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-336: The code, pre-trained models, filter coefficients, and synthetic audio samples used in our experiments are available at \url{https://github.com/blindconf/fingerprint .}
elements.sent-2411-337: To ensure a comprehensive evaluation of our attribution method across diverse synthesis techniques and deployment scenarios, we utilize multiple speech corpora and include synthetic speech generated by a wide range of systems.
elements.sent-2411-338: This setup allows us to assess model attribution performance under various architectural paradigms, and to evaluate generalization across different speakers, languages, and domains.
elements.sent-2411-339: We focus primarily on three datasets:.
elements.sent-2411-340: We use the LJSpeech corpus~\cite{ljspeech_2017 , a single-speaker English dataset comprising 13,100 audio clips.}
elements.sent-2411-341: Synthetic speech samples for this dataset are drawn from the WaveFake dataset~\cite{frank_2021_NeurIPS and its extension~\cite{gasenzer_2024_TMLR , which cover a wide range of speech synthesis systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: In addition, we augment the dataset with synthetic speech generated using publicly available implementations of diffusion-based models and hybrid models.
elements.sent-2411-343: Together, these systems span four major categories of generative speech technologies:.
elements.sent-2411-344: GAN-based models: These models learn to generate waveforms using adversarial training.
elements.sent-2411-345: We include MelGAN Large (MG-L)~\cite{kumar_2019_NEURIPS , Parallel WaveGAN (PWG)~\cite{Yamamoto_2020_ICASSP , Multi-band MelGAN (MB-MG)~\cite{yang_2021_SLT , HiFi-GAN (HF-G)~\cite{Kong_2020_NEURIPS , Avocodo (Avo)~\cite{bak_2023_AAAI , and BigVGAN (BVG)~\cite{lee_2023_ICLR .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-346: Flow-based models: These systems use invertible transformations to generate waveform distributions.
elements.sent-2411-348: Diffusion-based models: These generate speech through iterative denoising of noise samples.
elements.sent-2411-349: We include FastDiff and ProDiff~\cite{huang_2022_IJCAI, huang_2022_ACM , using their official pretrained models.\footnote{\url{https://github.com/Rongjiehuang/ProDiff .}}}
elements.sent-2411-350: Hybrid-based models: These combine signal processing techniques with neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-351: We include the Neural Source-Filter (NSF) model~\cite{wang_19_SSW .\footnote{\url{https://github.com/nii-yamagishilab/project-NN-Pytorch-scripts?tab=readme-ov-file .}}}
elements.sent-2411-352: This collection forms a reproducible benchmark covering all major generative paradigms in modern speech synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-353: To evaluate cross-lingual generalization, we use the JSUT corpus~\cite{JSUT_2017 , a single-speaker Japanese dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-354: Specifically, we use the basic5000 subcorpus, which contains 5,000 utterances.
elements.sent-2411-355: For synthetic counterparts, we utilize audio samples generated by PWG and MB-MelGAN models from the WaveFake dataset~\cite{frank_2021_NeurIPS , which are based on the basic5000.}
elements.sent-2411-356: This setup allows us to assess the robustness of attribution methods under language and domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-357: To evaluate attribution performance in a multi-speaker, open-world setting, we use the ASVspoof 2019 Logical Access (LA) corpus~\cite{Wang_2020_JournalCSL , which is derived from the Voice Cloning Toolkit (VCTK) dataset~\cite{veaux_2017_VCTK .}}
elements.sent-2411-358: VCTK contains recordings from 107 English speakers (46 male, 61 female) and is partitioned into disjoint training, development, and evaluation speaker sets.
elements.sent-2411-359: The ASVspoof LA corpus includes both genuine human speech (labeled as bonafide) and synthetically generated audio from a range of systems (labeled from A01 to A19).
elements.sent-2411-360: The training set consists of 2,580 genuine utterances and 22,800 synthetic utterances produced using four TTS and two VC systems.
elements.sent-2411-361: The development set follows a similar distribution, using the same synthetic systems as the training set.
elements.sent-2411-362: The evaluation set comprises 7,355 genuine utterances and 63,882 synthetic utterances generated using 13 different systems—of which 6 TTS and 5 VC systems are not present in the training set, while 1 TTS and 1 VC system are shared with it.
elements.sent-2411-363: In total, the dataset spans 17 different synthesis systems, making it a comprehensive benchmark for synthetic speech detection and model attribution in realistic, multi-speaker scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-365: We evaluate two attribution scoring functions—correlation-based and Mahalanobis distance-based—across multiple residual feature types, including EnCodec and spectral filtering (low-pass, high-pass, band-stop, and band-pass).
elements.sent-2411-366: All residual features are derived from STFT-based acoustic representations.
elements.sent-2411-367: For each feature type, we report the performance using its best-performing scoring function, selected based on the validation Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC).
elements.sent-2411-368: This allows a fair comparison between the EnCodec and the spectral filtering-based approach, each using its optimal configuration.

elements.sent-2411-371: EnCodec: Compression at 24~kbps, using the correlation-based score and an STFT window size of 2,048, and hop size of 128.
elements.sent-2411-372: Low-pass filter: Cutoff frequency at 1~kHz, with a stopband frequency at 1.5~kHz, using the Mahalanobis distance-based attribution score, and an STFT window size of 128, and a hop size of 2.
elements.sent-2411-373: For each target synthesis system, a fingerprint is computed using 80\% of its available samples.
elements.sent-2411-374: The remaining 20\% are used for testing on a balanced set composed of 50\% samples from the target system and 50\% from non-target sources (i.e., other synthesis systems or real speech).
elements.sent-2411-375: For the ASVspoof 2019 LA dataset, which is inherently imbalanced, we first combine the training and development sets (containing only categories A01–A06), and then split each into 80\% training and 20\% testing.
elements.sent-2411-376: To evaluate performance under open-world conditions with unseen synthesis types, we supplement the test set with data from the evaluation partition (A07–A19), sampling an equal number of test samples per category to match the amount taken from A01–A06 test splits.
elements.sent-2411-377: This yields a balanced test set that includes both known and unseen sources.
elements.sent-2411-378: We report the AUROC to quantify attribution performance without requiring a fixed decision threshold.

elements.sent-2411-448: Our neural-network–based fingerprint model, labeled Fingerprint (NN), outperforms all baselines in accuracy, F1, and precision, demonstrating the strong capability of learned residual representations for synthetic speech detection.
elements.sent-2411-449: Statistical significance tests using the Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test confirm that these improvements over all baselines are significant ($p < 0.05$).
elements.sent-2411-450: While our method also leads in recall, its advantage over the X-vector is less pronounced ($p = 0.117$).
elements.sent-2411-451: In addition, we evaluate a training-free version, labeled Fingerprint (Free).
elements.sent-2411-452: Despite its simplicity, this approach performs competitively—achieving an F1 score of 0.973 and surpassing several deep learning baselines in precision.
elements.sent-2411-453: This result highlights the robustness of the residual fingerprint representation itself.
elements.sent-2411-454: Moreover, we expect the performance of the training-free approach to improve further as the number and diversity of available fingerprints increase, potentially enhancing its generalization and detection capability.
elements.sent-2411-455: Together, these results highlight the inherent strength of the residual fingerprints themselves, and demonstrate the strong effectiveness of our approach for reliable synthetic speech detection.
elements.sent-2411-456: With speech generation models achieving remarkable realism and ease of use—or misuse—the need for effective spoofing detection and synthesis system attribution in both open- and closed-world settings is growing rapidly, making them critical components for securing voice-based communication systems, mitigating misinformation, and ensuring the integrity of biometric authentication.
elements.sent-2411-457: Our proposed fingerprinting approach—using either EnCodec or low-pass filtered residuals—demonstrates strong performance across a wide range of evaluation settings.
elements.sent-2411-458: Unlike most existing classification-based methods for audio deepfake detection, our approach is simple to implement and leverages lightweight signal-level artifacts rather than complex model training, offering a practical and scalable alternative to the model attribution and deepfake detection toolbox.
elements.sent-2411-459: Single-Model Attribution in an Open-World Setting: Our method achieves near-perfect AUROC scores when distinguishing samples generated by a target synthesis system from unknown systems and real audio, demonstrating strong robustness and generalization without extensive training.
elements.sent-2411-460: Multi-Model Attribution in a Closed-World Setting: Using residual fingerprints, our approach outperforms deep-learning baselines while remaining lightweight and interpretable, reliably identifying the correct synthesis source among known candidates.
elements.sent-2411-461: Real vs.~Synthetic Detection: Even in a training-free setup, our method effectively separates synthetic from real audio, highlighting the strength of residual fingerprints as a signal-level detection cue—particularly useful in low-resource or rapid-response scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-462: With minimal effort, performance can be further improved by training a simple neural network, achieving results that surpass current state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-463: Robustness: We further demonstrate that our single-model attribution framework proves effective in noise conditions, maintaining good performance (SNR~$\geq$~\SI{15 {\decibel ), which further underscores its practicality.}}
elements.sent-2411-464: Research implications: This work contributes to the growing field of audio deepfake forensics by emphasizing single-model open-world attribution, a realistic and highly challenging setting that remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-465: Unlike multi-class classification, where all possible synthesis systems are assumed known, open-world attribution more closely mirrors real-world scenarios—especially in adversarial settings where attackers can train and deploy proprietary models.
elements.sent-2411-466: Research in this direction can advance more generalizable and scalable forensic tools.
elements.sent-2411-467: Limitations: Our single-model attribution setting relies on some access to target samples to construct a reliable fingerprint.
elements.sent-2411-468: In scenarios where an attacker uses a privately trained or unreleased model, collecting such data may be challenging.
elements.sent-2411-469: Extensions: While we focus on low-pass and EnCodec-based residuals, our modular framework supports other signal decompositions.
elements.sent-2411-470: Appendix~\ref{appendix:additional-results shows that mid-frequency filters are also effective, suggesting room for task-specific tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Future work could examine the role of fingerprint robustness under transformations such as bandwidth variation, bit rate changes, or neural re-synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-472: Understanding how different synthesis systems leave distinct fingerprints may unlock further opportunities for model attribution, tampering detection, and content provenance.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \appendices.}

elements.sent-2411-499: We describe the network architectures, preprocessing methods, and relevant training details for reproducibility and clarity.
elements.sent-2411-500: We adopt a time-delay neural network (TDNN)-based X-vector model~\cite{Waibel_1989_IEEEJournal, Peddinti_2015_Interspeech , originally introduced by Snyder et al.~\cite{Snyder_2018_ICASSP .}}
elements.sent-2411-501: The model consists of five TDNN layers with context sizes $\{5, 3, 2, 1, 1\}$ and dilations $\{1, 1, 2, 1, 3\}$, each followed by ReLU activation and dropout ($p=0.5$).
elements.sent-2411-502: Statistical pooling (mean and standard deviation) is applied over the time dimension to produce a fixed 1,024-dimensional embedding, which is passed through a two-layer multi-layer perceptron (MLP) with 512 units per layer and finally mapped to class logits via a softmax output layer.
elements.sent-2411-503: We implement a Light Convolutional Neural Network (LCNN) following the design of Lavrentyeva et al.~\cite{lavrentyeva_2019_interspeech , which incorporates the Max-Feature-Map (MFM) activation~\cite{wu_2018_IEEE .}}
elements.sent-2411-504: The model consists of nine convolutional blocks with interleaved MFM activations and batch normalization.
elements.sent-2411-505: Selected blocks are followed by max-pooling for spatial downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-506: An adaptive max-pooling layer aggregates spatial features into a fixed-length vector, which is processed by a two-layer MLP with dropout ($p=0.75$), batch normalization, and MFM activation.
elements.sent-2411-507: A softmax output layer produces class logits.
elements.sent-2411-508: We implement a ResNet-18~\cite{Kaiming_2016_CVPR architecture, composed of four residual stages with 2, 2, 2, and 2 blocks respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-509: Each block contains two $3\times3$ convolutions with batch normalization and ReLU, and includes skip connections with optional downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-510: The network begins with a $7\times7$ convolution (stride 2), followed by batch normalization, ReLU, and max-pooling.
elements.sent-2411-511: After the residual stages, global average pooling generates a fixed-size feature vector, which is passed to a single MLP head for classification.
elements.sent-2411-512: We extend the ResNet-18 architecture by integrating Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks~\cite{Jie_2018_CVPR into each residual block.}
elements.sent-2411-513: Each SE module applies global average pooling followed by a two-layer MLP (with ReLU and sigmoid activations) to compute channel-wise scaling factors.
elements.sent-2411-514: These are used to reweight feature maps before the residual addition.
elements.sent-2411-515: The overall architecture mirrors that of ResNet-18, with downsampling performed via strided convolutions in the first block of each stage.
elements.sent-2411-516: The final global average pooled feature vector is fed into a classification MLP.
elements.sent-2411-517: We implement a ResNet-18-based architecture adapted from Deng et al.~\cite{Junlong_2024_ICASSP , modified to accept 16-channel input features.}
elements.sent-2411-518: After standard ResNet layers and four residual stages (\{64, 128, 256, 512\} channels), global average pooling produces a 512-dimensional feature vector.
elements.sent-2411-519: Two heads follow: (1) a classification head using a single fully connected layer, and (2) a projection head, which is a two-layer MLP (128 hidden units, 64 output), using ReLU activation and $\ell_2$ normalization for contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-520: Training uses a multi-task loss combining cross-entropy and supervised contrastive loss~\cite{Prannay_2020_NEURIPS , with weighting learned via the uncertainty-based method~\cite{Cipolla_2018_IEEE_CVF .}}
elements.sent-2411-521: Depending on the baseline model, different time-frequency representations are applied as inputs:.
elements.sent-2411-522: ResNet, SE-ResNet, LCNN, and X-vector: These models receive linear frequency cepstral coefficients (LFCCs) as input.
elements.sent-2411-523: LFCCs are extracted using 20 filters and 60 coefficients, with an STFT configured for a window length of 25 ms and a hop length of 10 Ms. A Hann window function is applied.
elements.sent-2411-524: VFD-Net: This model uses log-mel spectrograms with 80 mel bands, computed using an STFT with a 1200-sample window and 300-sample hop.
elements.sent-2411-525: A Hamming window function is applied.
